content.assessment: T5, the no-new-policy baseline, is not addressed by the Polish lens directly, since Poland's transformations were active legislative interventions rather than passive demographic drift. However, the lens's implementation-sequencing criterion is indirectly informative: Poland's 2017 experience shows that even active, planned structural change can generate severe disruption when transition is not phased; by extension, wholly unmanaged passive contraction (T5) carries an analogous but under-examined risk of uncoordinated disruption, albeit without the direct evidentiary basis to quantify it via Polish findings.
opportunities: None directly supported by Polish evidence, since Poland's experience concerns active reform
strengths: The Polish case illustrates that even government-planned structural transitions require careful phasing to avoid disruption; this reinforces that unmanaged, non-policy-driven passive contraction is unlikely to be self-correcting or coordinated (implied contrast with F-live-polish_reform-04, F-live-polish_reform-05)
threats: The absence of any structural mechanism or planned sequencing in T5 parallels the Polish 2017 lesson that unplanned transitions (even smaller ones) generate disruption; passive demographic contraction lacking a phasing plan could reproduce a diffuse version of the unplaced-student/closure problem seen in Poland, albeit through market rather than legislative mechanisms (F-live-polish_reform-05)
weaknesses: Poland's reform history is not directly evidentially portable to a scenario with no active policy at all, so the lens offers only indirect, low-confidence inference for T5
